exercises.title: Confidence Evidence Journal
exercises.description: For 30 days, keep a 'Confidence Evidence' journal. Each day, write: (1) One thing I did that required courage (even small), (2) One thing I learned from doing it, (3) One piece of evidence that I'm more capable than I think. At the end of the month, review for patterns.
hints: Start very small — even 'spoke up in a meeting' counts
Focus on the ACTION, not the outcome
Include evidence from others' positive reactions

solution.code:
Confidence Evidence Journal — Sample entries:

Day 3: Called the restaurant to make a reservation (anxiety about phone calls)
Lesson: It was straightforward; my anticipation was worse than reality.
Evidence: I handled it quickly and got exactly what I wanted.

Day 12: Shared a controversial opinion in team meeting
Lesson: People engaged thoughtfully; they didn't dismiss me.
Evidence: Two colleagues followed up to discuss my point further.

Day 21: Introduced myself to someone at a networking event
Lesson: Asking questions is easier than making statements.
Evidence: They said 'That's a really interesting perspective' about my work.

Month review:
- Total courage actions recorded: 28/30
- Most common anxiety trigger: Phone calls and public speaking
- Biggest growth area: Initiating conversations with strangers
- Key insight: 90% of my feared outcomes never happened
- Evidence pattern: Others are more receptive than I predict
- Confidence level: Started at 3/10, ended at 6/10 on public speaking

exercises.title: The 30-Day Challenge Escalator
exercises.description: Create a personal 30-day challenge with escalating difficulty in an area you want more confidence. Week 1: daily micro-challenges. Week 2: small challenges 3x/week. Week 3: medium challenges 2x/week. Week 4: one big challenge. Track completion and feelings.
hints: Choose ONE domain to focus on (social, professional, creative)
Define specific, measurable challenges for each week
Rate your anxiety before and after each challenge (1-10)

solution.code:
30-Day Confidence Challenge: Public Speaking

Week 1 (Micro-challenges, daily):
Day 1: Spoke first in meeting (anxiety: 6, after: 3)
Day 2: Gave opinion in group chat (anxiety: 3, after: 1)
Day 3: Asked question in webinar (anxiety: 5, after: 2)
Day 4: Recorded 1-min video about my work (anxiety: 7, after: 4)
Day 5: Shared article with personal commentary (anxiety: 4, after: 2)
Day 6: Left detailed comment on LinkedIn post (anxiety: 3, after: 1)
Day 7: Spoke up twice in meeting (anxiety: 4, after: 2)

Week 2 (Small challenges, 3x/week):
Day 9: 2-min presentation to manager (anxiety: 7, after: 3)
Day 11: Led part of team standup (anxiety: 6, after: 3)
Day 13: Gave feedback to colleague (anxiety: 5, after: 2)

Week 3 (Medium challenges, 2x/week):
Day 16: 5-min presentation to team (anxiety: 8, after: 4)
Day 20: Mentored new hire (anxiety: 6, after: 2)

Week 4 (Big challenge):
Day 28: Led all-hands presentation (anxiety: 9, after: 3)

Results:
- Average anxiety before: 5.8
- Average anxiety after: 2.5
- Anxiety reduction: 57%
- Completed 26/30 challenges (87%)
- Biggest insight: Anxiety peaks BEFORE the event, not during it

exercises.title: The Competence Inventory
exercises.description: Create a detailed inventory of your actual competencies — things you can do well. For each, provide specific evidence. This counters the impostor syndrome tendency to dismiss your abilities. Include: technical skills, interpersonal skills, unique experiences, and knowledge areas.
hints: Be specific — 'I can code in Python' is less powerful than 'I built X using Python'
Include evidence from others' feedback, not just self-assessment
Include skills others might not know about

solution.code:
Competence Inventory:

TECHNICAL SKILLS:
1. Python programming — Built automated data pipeline that saved 10 hours/week
2. Data analysis — Created dashboard that influenced Q3 strategy decisions
3. Project management — Led 3 successful projects on time and under budget

INTERPERSONAL SKILLS:
1. Active listening — Manager noted I 'make people feel heard' in 360 review
2. Conflict resolution — Mediated disagreement between two team members successfully
3. Mentoring — Two junior developers credited me with helping them get promoted

UNIQUE EXPERIENCES:
1. Led cross-functional team across 3 time zones
2. Presented to C-suite (terrifying but successful)
3. Survived and thrived through 2 company restructurings

KNOWLEDGE AREAS:
1. Industry expertise — Recognized as go-to person for [specific domain]
2. Regulatory knowledge — Only person on team who understands compliance requirements
3. Historical context — Know company's technical history, useful for decision-making

Evidence from others:
- 'You're the person I come to when I'm stuck' (3 colleagues)
- 'Your analysis is always thorough and actionable' (manager)
- 'You make complex topics simple' (stakeholder)

Impostor syndrome check: If 3+ independent sources confirm a skill, it's probably real.
